_provenance.source_pdf: Aebersold, Jamey - Vol 001, How to Play and Improvise Jazz.pdf

systems.name: Chord-Scale Equivalence System
systems.summary: The central system of this volume treats every chord symbol as simultaneously naming a chord (vertical) and a scale (horizontal), creating a unified taxonomy of harmonic-melodic materials organized by quality. Members are the chord/scale categories defined in the Scale Syllabus (Chapter 15): major, dominant 7th, minor (Dorian), half-diminished, and their altered variants. Within each category, scale choices are ordered by dissonance level from consonant to tense, placing the improviser's tension-and-release control directly in their scale selection. Traversal rules govern how the improviser moves between chord-scale members across a progression (guide-tone voice motion, common-tone continuity, and the ii-V bebop-scale substitution), while modification rules govern how a given chord-scale member can be altered or substituted (dissonance escalation within a category, pentatonic extraction, chromatic approach embellishment, and blues-scale overlay).

members.name: Major Scale / Chord (CΔ)
members.summary: Stable, consonant quality; 4th degree treated as passing tone.

members.name: Dominant 7th Scale / Chord (C7)
members.summary: Unstable quality resolving up a perfect 4th; 4th degree avoided; seven scale options from DOM.7th through Spanish/Jewish.

members.name: Dorian Minor Scale / Chord (C-)
members.summary: Universal minor quality; preferred over pure minor throughout the method.

members.name: Half-Diminished Scale / Chord (C∅)
members.summary: Used as the ii chord in minor ii-V progressions; bebop scale covers it jointly with the resolving dominant.

members.name: Minor with Major 7th (C-Δ)
members.summary: Minor chord with raised 7th; listed in reduced notation system.

members.name: Altered Dominant +9 (C7+9 / Diminished Whole Tone)
members.summary: Four altered tones (b9, +9, +4, +5); maximum tension dominant variant.

traversal_rules.name: Guide-Tone Voice Motion at Chord Changes
traversal_rules.summary: When moving from one chord-scale member to the next, prioritize the 3rd and 7th as melodic targets on beats 1 and 3, exploiting their tendency to resolve by half-step across chord boundaries.

references.quote_excerpt: the 3rd and 7th of scales/chords are the most important tones
Improvise on the 3rd or 7th of each chord
The two most important notes in any scale are the 3rd and 7th

traversal_rules.name: Common-Tone Continuity Across Scale Changes
traversal_rules.summary: When moving between chord-scale members, identify and lean on tones shared by both scales to create smooth, connected melodic transitions.

references.quote_excerpt: These are called common tones
moving from one chord symbol to another and playing logical musical phrases in a connected, smooth manner

traversal_rules.name: Dominant 7th Resolves Up a Perfect Fourth
traversal_rules.summary: When traversing from a dominant 7th chord-scale member, expect and target resolution to the chord-scale member a perfect 4th above, with altered tones moving by half-step to their targets.

references.quote_excerpt: Dominants typically want to resolve to a chord up a perfect 4th
Those tones usually resolve by half-step to a scale or chord tone

traversal_rules.name: ii-V Bebop Scale Substitution
traversal_rules.summary: The dominant 7th bebop scale covers both the minor ii chord and the dominant V chord in a ii-V progression, allowing a single scale to traverse both members.

references.topic: Dom 7th bebop scale as ii-chord substitute
references.quote_excerpt: The dom.7th bebop scale can act as a substitute for the minor ii chord

traversal_rules.name: Chromatic Approach to Chord Tones Across Boundaries
traversal_rules.summary: Approach each chord tone of the arriving chord-scale member from a half step below, from enclosure (scale tone above + chromatic tone below), or descending from a whole step above.

references.topic: Approach chord tones from half step below
references.quote_excerpt: approach each chord tone from a half step below

references.topic: Scale tone above + half step below
references.quote_excerpt: scale tone above each chord tone, and using the half step leading tone from below

references.topic: Leading into guide tones by half step
references.quote_excerpt: Lead into the 3rd or 7th by half step

traversal_rules.name: Circle of Fourths Root Movement
traversal_rules.summary: The harmonic foundation of jazz is root movement by perfect fourth; internalize and practice all chord-scale traversals through this cycle across all 12 keys.

references.quote_excerpt: the harmonic movement of a perfect fourth is found everywhere in jazz
practice their scales, chords, patterns and Bb licks through the Circle of Fourths

modification_rules.name: Dissonance Escalation Within Chord-Scale Category
modification_rules.summary: Within any chord-scale quality category, the improviser may substitute a more dissonant scale option from the Scale Syllabus list, moving downward from consonant to tense to increase harmonic tension.

references.quote_excerpt: scales near the top of each category will sound mild or consonant and scale choices further down the list will become increasingly tense or dissonant
Music is made of tension and release

modification_rules.name: Minor Scale Preference Order (Dorian First)
modification_rules.summary: For any minor chord-scale member, substitute scale choices in the order: Dorian, Bebop, Melodic, Blues, Pentatonic; Pure Minor is last resort.

references.quote_excerpt: Dorian, Bebop, Melodic, Blues, Pentatonic
Every minor scale employed on the record and in the musical examples is in the Dorian mode

modification_rules.name: Dominant 7th Scale Palette Expansion
modification_rules.summary: Over any dominant 7th chord-scale member, the player may substitute from seven available scales: DOM.7th, Bebop, Lydian Dominant, Whole-tone, Diminished, Diminished Whole-tone, and Spanish/Jewish.

references.quote_excerpt: BEBOP = C7 = CDEFGABbBC
LYDIAN DOM. = C7#4
Experiment with these scales over the Cycle of Dominant 7th Chords

modification_rules.name: Fourth Degree Omission / Passing-Tone Demotion
modification_rules.summary: In major and dominant 7th chord-scale members, the 4th scale degree must not be emphasized or landed upon; it is demoted to a passing tone between 3rd and 5th.

references.quote_excerpt: The 4th is usually treated as a passing tone between the 3rd and 5th
Be careful when playing the 4th note of major or dominant scales. Treat it as a passing tone.

modification_rules.name: Color Tone Addition — Major and Dominant
modification_rules.summary: Over major and dominant 7th chord-scale members, the 6th, 7th, 9th, and #4 are available as color tones that add expressive tension within the tension-release arc.

references.quote_excerpt: The pretty notes for major and dominant 7th chords/scales are the 6,7,9, and #4

modification_rules.name: Color Tone Addition — Minor
modification_rules.summary: Over minor chord-scale members (Dorian), the 4th, 6th, 7th, and 9th are available as color tones that create tension to be resolved.

references.quote_excerpt: The pretty notes for minor chords/scales are 4,6,7, and 9th

modification_rules.name: Pentatonic Subset Extraction
modification_rules.summary: Any chord-scale member may be modified by extracting one of its embedded pentatonic subsets for use as a lighter, sparser texture; the 4th degree is typically omitted from major-scale pentatonic subsets.

references.quote_excerpt: We usually avoid using the 4th note of the major scale as part of a pentatonic scale
sprinkle it in amongst other scale sounds

modification_rules.name: Blues Scale Overlay on Minor and Dominant Members
modification_rules.summary: Over static minor chords (2+ measures) or dominant 7th members, the blues scale may be overlaid as an intentionally clashing colorant; it must be deployed as extracted licks, not scalar runs.

references.quote_excerpt: The blues scale can also be used over minor chords when the minor chord is sounded for 2, 4, 8, or 16 measures

references.topic: Jazz Use of Blues Scale (Licks, Not Runs)
references.quote_excerpt: Jazz players usually play bits and pieces of the scale or make up licks
the notes of the blues scale often clash with the given harmony, but that is what makes it sound like the blues
alternate between the dorian minor and the blues scale

modification_rules.name: Bebop Chromatic Tone Insertion
modification_rules.summary: Any diatonic scale member may be modified by inserting a single chromatic passing tone to create an 8-tone bebop scale; the added tone must always fall on an upbeat.

references.quote_excerpt: The bebop scale contains one added tone to each of the four most used scales

references.topic: Added tone must land on upbeat
references.quote_excerpt: The added tone must always come on the upbeat

references.topic: 8-tone scale places chord tones on beat
references.quote_excerpt: Since the scale has 8 tones, it helps to naturally place the chord tones ON the beat

modification_rules.name: Twelve-Key Symmetry Reduction (36 → 12 Patterns)
modification_rules.summary: The 36 apparent scales in the syllabus reduce to 12 finger patterns because major, Dorian minor, and dominant 7th scales built on related roots share identical key signatures and fingerings.

references.quote_excerpt: The 36 scales on page 60/61 can be reduced to 12 scales or twelve finger patterns on your instrument
C major = D minor (dorian) = G7 (dominant 7th or mixolydian)

systems.name: Tension-Release Melodic Architecture System
systems.summary: The book's governing aesthetic system treats an improvised solo as a single dramatic arc built from parameterized tension and release events. Members are the expressive parameters the improviser controls: melodic direction, rhythmic density, register, interval width, harmonic consonance, dynamics, articulation, rhythmic placement, and silence. Traversal rules govern how the soloist moves through a solo arc — from statement through development to climax to release — and how rhythmic placement (ahead/on/behind the beat) shapes expressive character. Modification rules govern how any given moment's tension level is raised or lowered by manipulating individual parameters from the paired reference lists codified in Chapter 13.

members.name: Consonant Anchor Zone (Root, 3rd, 5th)
members.summary: Stable harmonic resting points that begin and end phrases and produce release.

members.name: Tension Zone (Non-Chord Tones, Color Tones, Passing Tones)
members.summary: Scale degrees and chromatic tones that produce instability requiring resolution.

members.name: Rhythmic Density Level
members.summary: Note-value stratum; faster values build tension, longer values release it.

members.name: Beat Placement (Ahead / On / Behind)
members.summary: Temporal relationship to the pulse; each placement carries a distinct expressive character.

members.name: Register Zone (Low / Middle / High / Extreme)
members.summary: Pitch region; extreme registers add tension.

members.name: Melodic Direction (Ascending / Descending / Mixed)
members.summary: Ascending builds tension; descending releases it.

members.name: Silence / Rest
members.summary: Structural space counted as part of the phrase.

traversal_rules.name: Solo Arc: Statement → Development → Climax → Release
traversal_rules.summary: The improviser traverses the solo arc in four stages: state a theme/motif, develop it, build to a climax, then release; each climax in a multi-chorus solo must exceed the previous one.

references.quote_excerpt: Statement of theme (motif), development of theme, climax, and release
direct the flow of your melodic line toward a natural climax and then immediately taper off
make each successive climax more dramatic than the previous one

traversal_rules.name: Beat Placement as Expressive Character Selector
traversal_rules.summary: Moving from on-the-beat to ahead-of-the-beat increases forward energy; moving behind-the-beat creates a laid-back feel; beginners must establish on-beat placement first.

references.quote_excerpt: Ahead of the beat (on top), On the beat (right on), Behind the beat (laying back)

references.topic: feel character of each beat placement
references.quote_excerpt: playing ahead of the beat usually implies excitement and forward motion
Beginning improvisors should learn to play on the beat

traversal_rules.name: Phrase Resolution Targets Strong Beats
traversal_rules.summary: Tension phrases resolve on beats 1 or 3 (or the 'and' of 2 or 4); this rhythmic landing rule governs how the soloist exits a tension zone and arrives at a consonant anchor.

references.quote_excerpt: natural to end on beats one or three, in 4/4 time
place (play) chord tones on the downbeat, especially beats one and three

modification_rules.name: Tension Parameter Manipulation (Raise / Lower)
modification_rules.summary: At any moment in the solo, the improviser raises tension by applying elements from the tension list or lowers it by applying release elements.

references.quote_excerpt: ELEMENTS WHICH PRODUCE TENSION ... ELEMENTS WHICH PRODUCE RELEASE
tension can be achieved through the manipulation of volume, direction of the melodic line, range, intervals

modification_rules.name: Rhythmic Density Shift for Tension/Release
modification_rules.summary: The improviser modifies the current tension level by shifting note-value density: eighth and sixteenth notes build tension; whole and half notes with rests create relaxation.

references.quote_excerpt: 8th and 16th notes build tension. Rests, whole and half-notes create relaxation

modification_rules.name: Silence as Structural Release Element
modification_rules.summary: Silence is not absence but a counted structural component of the phrase, deployable as a release tool and listener orientation device.

references.quote_excerpt: If you can't think of what should come next in a solo, try using silence
rests count as part of your phrase

modification_rules.name: Tension Resolution Method Selection
modification_rules.summary: A tension zone may resolve melodically (to a consonant chord tone), or by artificial means: abrupt dynamic change, tempo change, key change, or use of rests.

references.quote_excerpt: resolve naturally - melodically (to one of the consonant tones), or by artificial means

systems.name: Twelve-Key Memorization Practice System
systems.summary: This system governs how the student internalizes any scale, pattern, or progression across all 12 keys. The members are the ordered stages of the 12-step memorization procedure, organized as rungs of a progressive ladder. Traversal rules specify how the student advances through the rungs within a single key before moving to the next key, and how transposition proceeds. Modification rules cover ear-first internalization — singing before playing — and the adjustment of entry point based on the student's experience level. The telos is auditory anticipation: the state where the mind hears the progression before the fingers move.

members.name: Step 8 — Full Scale Up and Down
Step 10 — Scale Up to 9th, Back Down Chord Tones

traversal_rules.name: Sequential 12-Step Rung Progression
traversal_rules.summary: The student traverses the memorization ladder one rung at a time within each key, completing all 12 steps before declaring mastery; by step 12 the mind should be hearing the progression in advance.

references.quote_excerpt: 12. Play scale in broken thirds up and down. (1,3,2,4,3,5,4,6,5,7,6,8,7,9,8 then backwards)
By the twelfth chorus your mind will be HEARING the chord/scale progression in advance

traversal_rules.name: Transposition Through All 12 Keys via Root Sequences
traversal_rules.summary: After mastering a pattern in one key, transpose it through all 12 keys using one of the six prescribed root sequences (chromatic, Circle of Fourths, and others) to guarantee complete coverage.

references.topic: Root sequence practice in all keys
references.quote_excerpt: practice the above exercises, using as a guide any of the following root sequences
make sure you have mastered the pattern or lick in one key before moving on to the next key
memorize one example and then play it through all 12 keys

traversal_rules.name: Chromatic (Half-Step) Transposition Drill
traversal_rules.summary: Patterns may also be traversed chromatically — key by key in half-steps — as combined ear training and finger coordination exercise.

references.quote_excerpt: move (practice) patterns up and down my instrument chromatically (in half-steps)

modification_rules.name: Ear-First Internalization (Sing Before Play)
modification_rules.summary: At any rung of the memorization ladder, the student must first internalize the material aurally — singing the scale or pattern — before executing it on the instrument.

references.quote_excerpt: memorize the SOUND of the scales and chords
try singing the scale with your voice. Improvise with your voice over the scale you are learning and then play on your instrument what your voice sang
Use it to sing with then transfer the music to your instrument

modification_rules.name: Note-Value Entry Point Adjustment by Experience Level
modification_rules.summary: The student modifies the rhythmic density of practice at each rung based on their experience: beginners start with whole notes, advancing players with quarter or eighth notes.

references.quote_excerpt: A beginner may want to start with whole notes

modification_rules.name: Seven-Step Scale Practice Algorithm
modification_rules.summary: Within a single key, the student applies the seven-step practice sequence as a compression of the full 12-step procedure for initial scale introduction.

references.quote_excerpt: Play the scale from root to the 9th and back down slowly, slurred, several times
